title: Chapter 4: The Hollow Cathedral
content: Access to the Hollow Cathedral was through the Fine Arts building's basement — the same basement where Roxanne had nearly died twelve hours ago.
She stood at the top of the stairs now, holding a flashlight she'd found in the janitor's closet. The Executioner stood behind her. Lena stood next to her, sketchbook clutched to her chest.
"You don't have to come," Roxanne said.
"You're walking into a sealed basement with a god in it. I'm not letting you do that alone."
"I have the Executioner."
"The Executioner doesn't have a sketchbook. If there's a god down there, someone should draw it."
Roxanne laughed. "Fair point."
They descended.

The stairs went deeper than the building's above-ground footprint should have allowed. The hallway stretched, twisted, and kept going. The walls changed from campus concrete to older stone — rough-hewn granite, then something darker, smoother, almost organic.
"The architecture is wrong," Lena whispered. "This doesn't match the building plans. The building plans don't go this deep."
"The Nether-Grid doesn't care about building plans."
The stairs ended at an iron door.

It was massive — twelve feet tall, eight feet wide, covered in symbols that Roxanne didn't recognize but somehow understood. They were prices. Contracts. The door itself was a legal document, written in the language of the Nether-Grid.
*SEAL OF THE BOUND GOD. DEPOSIT REQUIRED FOR ENTRY: 50,000 OBOLS. NON-REFUNDABLE.*
Roxanne whistled.
"Fifty thousand just to open the door."
She held out the card.
*PAYMENT: 50,000 OBOLS. ACCESS: HOLLOW CATHEDRAL — LOWER SANCTUM.*
The card pulsed. The iron door groaned — a sound like a ship hull buckling — and swung inward.
The darkness that spilled out wasn't normal darkness. It was thick. Heavy. It pressed against Roxanne's skin like cold water.
"Okay," Lena said, her voice tiny. "That's not regular dark."
"It's Nether-dark. It's supposed to feel like that."
"It feels like being swallowed."
"That's the marketing."
Roxanne stepped through.

The space opened up beyond the door. The Hollow Cathedral wasn't a room — it was a cavity. A negative space carved into reality itself. Pillars of fused crystal rose toward a ceiling that didn't exist; they just faded into violet-black fog above. The floor was polished obsidian, reflecting the faint glow of Roxanne's card.
And at the center of it all, suspended in a cage of chains that weren't made of metal but of crystallized light —
A man.
Or what looked like a man.

He was pale. Silver hair, so long it pooled beneath him like liquid mercury. His eyes were closed. His features were sharp, elegant, almost too perfect — the kind of face you'd expect to see in a pre-Raphaelite painting, not at the bottom of an art school basement.
He wore nothing but shadows. They clung to him like a tailored suit, moving slightly as if they were alive.
And he was chained. Chains wrapped around his wrists, his ankles, his chest. Each chain was inscribed with symbols that burned faintly gold whenever they touched his skin.
He didn't move when they entered.
But his lips parted.
"You're early," he said. His voice was quiet, resonant, like a cello played in a cathedral. "I wasn't expecting the heir for another three centuries."
Roxanne stopped.
"You knew I was coming?"

"I felt the card activate." His eyes were still closed. "Every being in the Nether-Grid felt it. But most of them thought it was a malfunction. A glitch. The Primordial Bloodline has been dormant for so long, they assumed it was extinct."
"But you didn't."
"I've been waiting longer than anyone."
He opened his eyes.
They were red. Not the red of blood — the red of dying stars. Deep. Ancient. They fixed on her with an intensity that made Roxanne's breath catch.
"You're the heir," he said. It wasn't a question.
"I'm an art student with a very strange credit card."
"Same thing."
He tilted his head. The chains shifted, the golden symbols flaring where they touched his skin.
"Do you know what you're holding?"
"A black card with infinite balance."

"It's a birthright," he said. "Forged from the heart of the Primordial Void. Passed down through the bloodline that was supposed to guard it. Your ancestors held it for twelve thousand years. Then they died out. Or so everyone thought."
He looked at her. Through her.
"But here you are. An art student with a maxed-out credit card, holding the single most valuable object in existence."
Roxanne's jaw tightened.
"How do you know about my credit card debt?"
"I can see contracts. Debts. Promises. Your card debt was a small one, but it was still a chain. The Primordial Card broke it."
There was a pause.
"You're Silas," Roxanne said. "The Bound God. The one they sealed here before humanity existed."
"Before this version of humanity, yes. There were others."
"Why did they seal you?"
Silas's eyes flickered. Something passed across his face — too fast to read. Pain? Regret? Anger?

"Because I refused to let them consume this world. The other Primordials wanted to harvest it — drain it of all life, all potential, all future. I said no. They called me a traitor. They bound me here and left me to starve."
He smiled. It wasn't a happy expression.
"That was four billion years ago. Give or take."
Lena was furiously sketching behind Roxanne, charcoal flying across paper.
"Roxy. Roxy, this is — do you realize what this is? He's a pre-human deity. A real one. I need better paper."
"Lena, focus."
"I am focused! I'm documenting!"
Roxanne turned back to Silas. The Bound God was watching her with those ancient red eyes, and there was something almost amused in his expression.
"Your friend has the right instinct," he said. "Document everything. This world won't last long if the other Primordials wake up."
"How do I stop them?"
"You don't. They're too old, too powerful. You survive them. And to survive them, you need leverage."
"Leverage like what?"
He pulled against the chains. The golden symbols blazed. The chains held.
"Leverage like me."
Roxanne's card pulsed.
She looked at the chains. The symbols. The price that flickered at the edge of her vision:
*CHAIN OF THE BOUND GOD — NULL-IRON SEAL — BUYOUT PRICE: 100,000,000 OBOLS*
One hundred million.
She looked at her card.
Balance: ∞.
"One hundred million," she said.
"A bargain," Silas replied, with the faintest trace of a smile. "I was worth considerably more before the war."
"I don't have one hundred million."
"You have infinity. Which part of that is confusing?"
"The part where I buy a god I just met."
"You bought a Chainsaw Executioner you'd known for approximately thirty seconds. I'm hurt by the double standard."
Roxanne stared at him.
"Did you just — make a joke?"
"I've been alone for four billion years. I've had time to work on my material."

She laughed. She couldn't help it. The absurdity of the situation — standing in a cathedral-sized hole in reality, negotiating with a chained god who had better comedic timing than most of her Tinder dates.
"Okay," she said. "I'm not buying you yet. But I'm going to think about it."
"Take your time. It's not like I'm going anywhere."
She turned to leave. Then stopped.
"Silas."
"Yes?"
"If I buy you — if I pay one hundred million Obols for your freedom — what do I get?"
He was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was different. Softer.

"You get my absolute loyalty. My protection. My service, for as long as you hold my contract. I will not betray you, not out of fear, not out of greed, not for any reason. The Null-Iron Chains bind my power, but a freely given contract binds my will."
"And if I don't buy you?"
"Then I wait for someone who will. Or I wait for the seals to decay. Either way, I will eventually be free. The question is whose side I'm on when that happens."
Roxanne met his eyes.
"I'll think about it," she said again.
But she already knew the answer.
*End of Chapter 4*
